questions.question: Explain the difference between hard locks and soft locks in file sharing. Give one example of where each might be used.
keyPoints: Hard locks (mandatory) are enforced by OS and cannot be ignored by processes
Soft locks (advisory) are suggestions that processes can choose to follow or ignore
Hard locks used in systems requiring strict data integrity
Soft locks used where flexibility is preferred over strict enforcement

questions.question: What is a race condition in file sharing? How can it be prevented?
keyPoints: Race condition occurs when multiple processes access/modify file concurrently
Final outcome depends on unpredictable execution order
Prevention methods: file locking, mutual exclusion, synchronization primitives
Can lead to data inconsistency or corruption if not controlled

questions.question: Describe the readers-writers problem in the context of file sharing. How is it typically solved?
keyPoints: Multiple readers can access file simultaneously (no modification risk)
Writers need exclusive access (modify data, must not be interrupted)
Solution: allow multiple shared locks, require exclusive lock for writes
Use reader-writer locks or semaphore-based solutions

questions.question: What are the four necessary conditions for deadlock in file sharing?
keyPoints: Mutual Exclusion: file can only be accessed by one process at a time
Hold and Wait: processes hold locks while waiting for additional locks
No Preemption: locks cannot be forcibly taken away from processes
Circular Wait: processes wait in a circular chain for each other's resources

questions.question: Explain the concept of NFS (Network File System) and its role in file sharing.
keyPoints: NFS is a distributed file system protocol
Allows remote files to be accessed as if stored locally
Enables collaboration across networked computers
Uses RPC for client-server communication
Files stored on server, accessed by multiple clients over network

questions.question: What is the difference between shared locks and exclusive locks in file systems?
keyPoints: Shared lock (read lock): allows multiple processes to read simultaneously
Exclusive lock (write lock): allows only one process to access file
Shared locks cannot coexist with exclusive locks
Exclusive locks block all other locks until released

questions.question: Explain Unix file sharing semantics. How do changes made by one process affect other processes accessing the same file?
keyPoints: Unix uses open file description model
Each process opening a file gets its own file descriptor
Changes by one process are immediately visible to others with file open
Writes are serialized by the OS
File offset is shared among processes sharing same file description

questions.question: Discuss the various mechanisms for implementing file sharing in multi-user operating systems. Explain their advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: File Locking: mandatory and advisory locks
Semaphores: synchronization primitives for resource access
Access Control Lists (ACLs): permission-based sharing
Unix file permissions: owner/group/others model
Network File Systems: distributed sharing (NFS, SMB)
Copy-on-Write: creates private copies on modification
Compare advantages/disadvantages of each approach

questions.question: Explain the deadlock problem in file sharing. How can deadlock be prevented, avoided, or detected?
keyPoints: Deadlock definition: circular waiting for resources
Prevention: eliminate one of the four conditions (break circular wait, no hold-wait, etc.)
Avoidance: use banker algorithm or resource allocation graph
Detection: periodically check for circular wait conditions
Recovery: terminate processes or preempt resources
Practical approaches: timeout mechanisms, lock ordering

questions.question: Describe the different approaches to consistency in distributed file sharing systems. Discuss the challenges involved.
keyPoints: Strong consistency: all changes immediately visible to all clients
Weak consistency: changes propagate eventually
Cache coherency protocols
Server-based vs distributed locking
Network partition challenges (CAP theorem)
Optimistic vs pessimistic concurrency control
File versioning and conflict resolution

questions.question: What is the difference between Unix and Windows file sharing semantics? Discuss the implications for application design.
keyPoints: Unix: open file description model, immediate visibility of changes
Windows: default is separate file views, explicit sharing mode required
Unix: shared file offset among processes with same descriptor
Windows: each process maintains separate file pointer by default
Implications: data consistency, locking behavior, application portability
